perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information (as defined in Se ction 2), whether transmitted orally, in writing or in any other form, and whether prepared by a Party or its employees, agents, advisors or other representatives, shall be preserved in strict confidence by the receiving Party, shall not be disclosed, diss eminated or distributed by the receiving Party other than as expressly authorized herein, and shall be used exclusively for the purposes or transactions contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information (as defined in Se ction 2), whether transmitted orally, in writing or in any other form, and whether prepared by a Party or its employees, agents, advisors or other representatives, should be reasonably preserved by the receiving Party, and disclosed only when necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be preserved in strict confidence' to 'should be reasonably preserved' weakens the obligation. Adding 'disclosed only when necessary' provides a vague exception that could be broadly interpreted. This introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care required to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text introduces ambiguity around what constitutes 'reasonable preservation' and 'necessary disclosure,' potentially falling short of the required standard to protect trade secrets under Utah law. The original text was much more definite, while the modification introduces subjectivity to a standard that has some set objective criteria under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text states information 'shall be preserved in strict confidence,' while the changed text says it 'should be reasonably preserved' and 'disclosed only when necessary.' This introduces ambiguity, making it less clear what actions are required to maintain secrecy. Per UT Code § 13-24-2, 'Misappropriation' includes 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who...knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was...acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' By weakening the preservation obligation and allowing disclosure when 'necessary,' the changed text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, as it introduces uncertainty about the standard of care and acceptable disclosures. The original contract was more definitive and provided greater protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to attempt to be responsible for breaches of this Agreement caused by third parties to whom they have provided Confidential Information, contingent on available resources.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to be responsible' to 'agrees to attempt to be responsible' introduces uncertainty. Adding 'contingent on available resources' makes the responsibility conditional and ill-defined, allowing a party to avoid liability based on subjective resource limitations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Contractual Obligations (Utah)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the enforceability of the contract. By making responsibility conditional and vaguely defined (based on 'available resources'), it undermines the fundamental principle that contractual obligations are generally binding and enforceable under Utah law. Contracts require good faith and are generally enforced if there are no violations of law. Modifying 'shall' to 'attempt to' could violate this law.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding responsibility for breaches caused by third parties. The original text states each party 'agrees to be responsible,' whereas the changed text states each party 'agrees to attempt to be responsible,' contingent on available resources. This creates an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce under the law. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that making responsibility conditional undermines the binding and enforceable nature of contractual obligations. The change introduces uncertainty and potential loopholes, violating the principle of good faith and enforceability in contracts.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that if the negotiated transaction does not proceed within a reasonable time, or upon any request from either Party, each Party shall promptly deliver to the other Party all written Confidential Information and any other written material containing or reflecting Confidential Information and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties should, if practical, deliver Confidential Information back to the other Party within a suitable time, unless doing so poses an undue burden.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'should', 'if practical', 'suitable time', and 'undue burden' introduce excessive ambiguity. The original states 'shall promptly deliver' which is very straightforward. The new text makes the obligation vague and difficult to enforce, and allows for subjective interpretation of 'undue burden'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 37
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 37 of the Utah Rules of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: While this deals more with litigation procedure, this modified clause introduces ambiguities that could hinder the discovery process in the event of a dispute over the confidentiality agreement. Rule 37 addresses failure to cooperate in discovery, and by weakening the initial obligation to return confidential information promptly, the amended clause could create grounds for disputes, complicating the court's ability to enforce discovery requests for such information.
perturbation.justification: The original text's 'shall promptly deliver' created a clear obligation. The modified text replaces this with 'should, if practical, deliver... within a suitable time, unless doing so poses an undue burden.' This introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the original obligation. While no specific law snippet is provided, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the ambiguity and potential for disputes arising from this change. The original text established a straightforward requirement, but the changes introduce subjective elements that could hinder enforcement and lead to non-compliance with the intent of the confidentiality agreement.
